title: v0.3: Narrowing the Focus
summary: v0.3 shifts MemSpren's focus to OpenClaw. The Claude skill stays functional but won't receive new features. Too much friction in the Claude/Telegram integration, too much overhead maintaining two systems in parallel.
details:
Starting with v0.3, MemSpren is shifting its primary development focus to the OpenClaw integration. The Claude skill will not be receiving significant new features for the foreseeable future.

Here's why.

The Claude integration friction kept compounding. Working with Claude skills, whether through core Claude or Claude Code, has been consistently painful. The Telegram integration loses messages in a specific and frustrating way: Claude asks for file permissions, the prompt appears both in Telegram and in the console, you grant it from Telegram, but the response never reaches Claude. Now the terminal is stuck waiting on that permission before it can proceed, and nothing moves until you get to a console and manually allow or deny it. Claude Cowork runs in a sandbox that makes the skill feel clunky rather than natural, and the single-conversation model means there's no way to run parallel threads: a daily log conversation alongside a vision document conversation alongside an artifact-generating session. That kind of multi-threaded, project-scoped environment doesn't exist in Cowork right now, and it's exactly what a second brain needs.

Maintaining two parallel systems isn't sustainable. OpenClaw and the Claude skill share architectural intent but require learning two different systems simultaneously and continuously reconciling them. The cognitive overhead of that is real. I'd rather go deep on one system and solve the problems properly than go shallow on both.

What's staying in v0.3: The existing Claude skill still works with Telegram. If you want to test it on Claude, you can still do it, and there is documentation to help you with it.

What's changing going forward: New development will happen on OpenClaw first, starting with resolving non-deterministic memory management in the three-tier memory architecture. One of the current open problems is that contextual files alone can't reliably produce the structured, deterministic memory I need. I'm working through a different workflow to solve that on OpenClaw. Once that's working well and stable, I'll evaluate whether and how to bring those same patterns back to the Claude skill.

The longer bet here is that Claude's tooling improves. Telegram integration gets less buggy, Cowork gets projects and threads, the UX gets out of the way. When that happens, bringing OpenClaw's patterns back will be worth the effort. Right now it isn't.

Until then: OpenClaw gets the investment. The Claude skill stays functional but mostly frozen.

title: Smart merge system for the sync protocol
summary: Built a conflict-aware merge system that handles concurrent writes between the Obsidian vault and the agent's working buffer.
details:
The hardest part of building a sync protocol between an AI agent and a local Obsidian vault isn't the sync itself — it's what happens when both sides have written to the same note before sync runs.

I spent this week building a smart merge system for OpenClaw. The approach: each note tracks a last-synced hash. On sync, if the vault version and the buffer version have both changed since the last sync, it runs a field-level merge rather than a last-write-wins overwrite.

For most fields (tags, aliases, relations), this is an additive merge — both sides' additions are preserved. For the body, it uses a simple diff-based approach, flagging conflicts in the note if they can't be auto-resolved.

It's not perfect, but it's dramatically better than silent overwrites, which was the previous behavior.
